text: Hello, Adam Roth from LGN presenting another robotic sigmoid colectomy for adenocarcinoma of the mid sigmoid colon. This case was pretty tough and it was all related to the patient's BMI and body habitus in general. He was a BMI of forty-one but it was all in his belly. So as you'll see there's just a tremendous amount of visceral fat and I had trouble with retraction and even elevating the colon to put tension on the mesentery without things tearing. And then of course keeping the small bowel up out of the pelvis and away from the area that you're trying to dissect when you're doing the medial to lateral is challenging too as you will see.

So here I'm just kind of trying to restore normal anatomy. Mark Solomon would say that that's kind of the first step of the operation I think. And then like I said in the other videos, I do like to do some of the lateral mobilization, identify that ureter even laterally before heading to the medial to lateral dissection, especially in a case like this where the mesenteric fat is so abundant.

The surgeon provides a view of the contracted tumor located in the middle of the sigmoid colon, avoiding direct contact with the tumor. The sigmoid colon below the tumor extends to the rectosigmoid junction, surrounded by abundant visceral fat. The assistant uses the fourth robotic arm to pull the tissue upward towards the patient's head, aiding in retraction.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon, navigating through the abundant mesenteric fat while carefully retracting surrounding tissues.
The surgeon connects the lateral and medial dissections, ensuring the ureter is identified and protected. The use of stents or ICG in the ureters is mentioned as a selective practice in these cases.

With this case, I kind of knew that the tumor wasn't involving the pelvic sidewall or anywhere near the ureter. And he had not had diverticulitis before, so I felt pretty comfortable that we were going to be able to identify it. in spite of his body habitus.
The surgeon maneuvers around the surgical field without yet addressing the vessels, indicating the proximal resection margin while navigating the challenging medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon flips the colon medially and navigates through a large amount of mesenteric fat to access the proximal colon. The inferior mesenteric artery is ligated, with careful attention to avoid losing track of the vessels and causing bleeding. The surgeon progresses towards the proximal resection margin.

The surgeon, having taken the vessels and reached the proximal colon, returns to the rectosigmoid junction to select the distal resection margin. The dissection is taken lower than necessary for transection to aid in anastomosis, ensuring mobility and alignment of the rectum. The mesentery is meticulously cleaned off to prevent it from being incorporated into the staple line. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used for visualization.

The surgeon confirms the proximal and distal perfusion and proceeds to make a Pfannenstiel incision to extract the specimen, which remains attached proximally. A purse string suture is placed, and the anvil is positioned. Due to the large incision required for the specimen, the assistant inserts a hand through a gel port to assist with the anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and performs a leak test using a rigid proctoscope, ensuring the anastomosis is open and not too tight.

All right, well, just going to clean up here a little bit, bring the momentum back down on top of the bowel. So I really appreciate you watching. The things I'm looking to kind of get from any feedback here would be, how do you deal with these people that have just so much intra-abdominal fat? The colon is very heavy. The mesentery is very thick. So what instruments do you use to retract? What do you grab when you're retracting? How do you utilize your assistant better than I did? And then keeping the small bowel up out of the pelvis. Those things would be really helpful if you have any advice. Thanks again.
